analysis:
- Algorithmic families: best performers split into (A) greedy exact-contribution (GHSS, GSI_LS, GAHSS variants) and (B) population/metaheuristic search (TPOSS, SPESS). Greedy methods win on solution quality per evaluation; metaheuristics explore more but pay heavy runtime.
- Cost drivers: repeated full hypervolume (HV) recomputation is the dominant cost in almost every heuristic (per-candidate or per-removal loops). GSI_LS recomputes HV for every candidate removal; GHSS/GAHSS compute HV_after per shortlist element without caching.
- Good tradeoffs: Monte‑Carlo sampling (Heuristic 5) and shortlist/proxy ranking (Heuristics 7 and 9) drastically reduce HV calls by replacing many exact evaluations with cheap approximations or small exact subproblems.
- Missed reuse: few implementations cache HVs, incremental contributions, or reuse dominance/skylines. Several recreate large arrays, convert types, or perform Python-level nested loops (distance, dominance checks), adding avoidable overhead.
- Complexity/overhead: tensor/weight-vector methods (GL_HSS, GAHSS) add significant algorithmic complexity and heavy precomputation; they may help directional selection but also increase implementation and runtime cost without clear reuse of HV results.
- Local search: small-budget swap LS (used in several heuristics) yields useful fine-tuning; its cost is acceptable if HV evaluations are restricted to a short candidate list.
- Practical wins: (1) prefilter dominated points and clamp candidate set size; (2) use cheap proxies (box volume, sum of coords, distance) to shortlist; (3) approximate contributions (Monte Carlo or cached dominance masks) and only run exact HV on the shortlist; (4) cache incremental HV and exploit additivity/dominance to avoid rebuilds.
experience: Prefer a hybrid: fast nondominated filtering + inexpensive proxy ranking to build a small shortlist, then incremental/cached exact HV evaluations for greedy picks, with Monte‑Carlo or dominance masks as fallback. Limit exact HV calls and budget local swaps; vectorize dominance checks and reuse computations.
current_reflection: Prefer a hybrid: fast nondominated filtering + inexpensive proxy ranking to build a small shortlist, then incremental/cached exact HV evaluations for greedy picks, with Monte‑Carlo or dominance masks as fallback. Limit exact HV calls and budget local swaps; vectorize dominance checks and reuse computations.
good_reflections: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: hybrid, nondominated filter, proxy ranking, cached/incremental HV, Monte‑Carlo, vectorization, shortlist, local swaps
- Advice: Filter dominated points, compute inexpensive proxy scores (vectorized MC or tensor proxies) to form a small shortlist, then apply incremental/cached exact HV for greedy picks and a limited swap-based local search; batch and reuse buffers.
- Avoid: Running full exact HV on all candidates, rebuilding objects inside loops, unvectorized dominance checks, or unlimited swap/evaluation budgets.
- Explanation: This minimizes expensive HV calls and redundant work while retaining exactness for top candidates; vectorization and caching make shortlist selection and incremental updates efficient.
